water_profiles.name: Balanced Profile
water_profiles.description: Suitable for beers ranging from dark golden to deep amber in color. The generally low ion content should not interfere with the taste of the beer.

water_profiles.name: Balanced Profile II
water_profiles.description: Similar to the Balanced Profile but with twice the minerals. Suitable for dark golden to deep amber beers. Higher ion content makes it better suited for stronger and more robust styles.

water_profiles.name: Burton on Trent (historic and decarbonated)
water_profiles.description: Known for very high sulfate content. Constructed from a historic water profile after decarbonation through boiling or slaked lime. Low residual alkalinity allowed English brewers to brew pale beers.

water_profiles.name: Burton on Trent (historic)
water_profiles.description: Known for water with very high sulfate content. Constructed from a water analysis of Burton well water published in 'Burton-on-Trent, Its History, Its Waters and Its Breweries' from 1869.

water_profiles.name: Dortmund (historic and decarbonated)
water_profiles.description: Dortmunder water after decarbonation with slaked lime. Resembles the decarbonated water profile given by Kolbach in his 1953 article and was most likely the water used by Dortmunder brewers.

water_profiles.name: Dortmund (historic)
water_profiles.description: High calcium and sulfate content which lend the Dortmunder Export additional bitterness. Reconstructed from analysis data given in a 1953 article about residual alkalinity by Kolbach.

water_profiles.name: Dublin (Dry Stout)
water_profiles.description: With its high alkalinity Dublin water is well suited for stouts and other dark ales.

water_profiles.name: Düsseldorf (Altbier)
water_profiles.description: The water profile of Düsseldorf (Germany) is well suited for Altbier and is very likely the water used by Altbier breweries without any modification. Ion levels taken from a 2013 water quality report.

water_profiles.name: Edinburgh (Scottish Ale, Malty Ale)
water_profiles.description: One of the historic water profiles for Edinburgh, Scotland. Because of its geology Scottish brewers have access to many different waters. This particular profile is well suited for darker Scottish ales.

water_profiles.name: Light Colored and Hoppy
water_profiles.description: Low residual alkalinity and a sulfate to chloride ratio balanced towards sulfate make this an excellent choice for light colored (2–5 SRM) and hop forward beers. The mineral level is restrained and should not show through in the taste.

water_profiles.name: Light Colored and Malty
water_profiles.description: Low residual alkalinity and a sulfate to chloride ratio balanced towards chloride make this an excellent choice for light colored (2–5 SRM) and malt forward beers. The mineral level is restrained and should not show through in the taste.

water_profiles.name: London (Porter, Dark Ales)
water_profiles.description: With its high temporary hardness the London water profile is well suited for dark beers like Porter. Taken from an average London water report and also matches the historic London profile found in various literature sources.

water_profiles.name: Munich (Dark Lager)
water_profiles.description: Munich water is high in temporary hardness and well suited for dark lagers such as Munich Dunkel, Schwarzbier or Doppelbock. Because of its high residual alkalinity, not recommended for Munich Helles or Maibock. Mineral levels from the 2013 Munich water quality report.

water_profiles.name: Munich (Decarbonated)
water_profiles.description: Munich water after treatment with slaked lime and the addition of calcium chloride and gypsum. The kind of water treatment a Munich brewer might do for lighter beers. Well suited for Märzen and Maibock.

water_profiles.name: Pilsen (Light Lager)
water_profiles.description: Very low mineral water, the traditional water for Bohemian pilsner beers. Despite its low calcium content, great pilsner and Helles style beers can be brewed with it.

water_profiles.name: Vienna
water_profiles.description: The classic Vienna water profile is moderately hard with notable carbonate levels. Well suited for Vienna Lager and other amber lager styles that benefit from a balanced, malty character.

water_profiles.name: Chimay
water_profiles.description: The water profile of the Chimay brewery in Belgium. High carbonate levels well suited for traditional Belgian abbey styles that benefit from higher alkalinity.
